goals.description:
Goal 1: Engagement
Magnolia School District will provide a robust Multi-Tiered System of Support (MTSS) that promotes
meaningful engagement by parents and students, ensures positive and inclusive school climates
that cultivate social-emotional and academic competencies, and nurtures the development of
healthy children and families.

actions.title:
School Office
Support
actions.description:
All school offices will be staffed with at least one bilingual school office
managers and/or assistant to support home-school communications and
parent outreach. This action is principally directed to English Learners as
a result of the state data demonstrating that all nine elementary schools
meet the 15% and above translation needs in Spanish and one school in
Vietnamese as measured by the CA Department of Education
(dq.cde.ca.gov/dataquest/). The Magnolia School District percentages
range from 16.85% - 57.34% . The goal of this action is to meet the
desired outcome in the self-reflection tool on the CA School Dashboard by
increasing the percentage from the 2024-25 engagement survey results by
10% on the annual survey.

actions.title: Office Staff Training
actions.description:
S chool office teams will receive annual training to support customer
service skills and understanding the needs of families. This action is
principally directed to low-income families as a result of the research that
suggests that students of poverty do not always feel comfortable
approaching school staff. A positive school climate provides higher levels
of communication, connection, and collaboration and is measured through
the CA School Dashboard (Priority 6) and District surveys. The goal of this
action is to meet the desired outcome in the self-reflection tool on the CA
School Dashboard by increasing percentages from the 2024-25 parent
engagement survey results by 10% on the annual survey.

actions.title:
Bilingual Parent
Involvement Liaisons
(Spanish and
Vietnamese)
actions.description:
All sites are staffed with a bilingual parent liaison to support family
outreach and coordinate parent education programs. Two District-level
Vietnamese liaisons support engagement efforts at all school sites. This
action is principally directed to English Learners as a result of the state
data demonstrating that all nine elementary schools meet the 15% and
above translation needs in Spanish and one school in Vietnamese as
measured by the CA Department of Education (dq.cde.ca.gov/dataquest/).
The Magnolia School District percentages range from 16.85% - 57.34% .
The goal of this action is to meet the desired outcome in the self-reflection
tool on the CA School Dashboard by increasing percentage from the 2024-
25 parent engagement survey results by 10% on the annual survey.

actions.title:
Parent Involvement
Programs
actions.description:
Parent workshops and classes are offered at every school site at least
once per trimester. Trainings are conducted by Parent Involvement
Liaisons (PILs) and other team members or contracted services as needed
to support delivery of parent education classes. Materials are purchased
for specific program offerings as needed.
In addition to parent involvement programs provided by PILs, District and
school-level staff will conduct specialized meetings annually to support
parents of English Learners (EL), low-income students (LI), and Foster
Youth (FY) and Students with Disabilities (SWD) to ensure that they have
access to specialized services and supports. District Committee
membership (DELAC/DPAC and LCAP Committee) will be reviewed to
ensure that EL, LI, and FY are represented and have input in the decision-
making process.
Parent orientation/information meetings will be conducted annually to
provide information on targeted classroom services and the specialized
student academy that is provided each year for students with exceptional
needs. District Committee membership (DELAC/DPAC and LCAP
Committee) will be reviewed to ensure that students with exceptional
needs are considered during the decision-making process.
This action is principally directed to low-income, English learner, and
Foster Youth families to support high levels of parent involvement
opportunities for unduplicated families. Magnolia School District has
80.5% of the families experiencing poverty and research shows that these
families do not always feel comfortable asking for help or assistance. In
addition, 0.5% of our students are Foster Youth, 40.9% of our students are
English Learners and all nine of our elementary schools meet the 15% and
above translation needs as measured by the CA Department of Education
and this action provides second language families with supports and
strategies on how to help their children with academic success by being
involved in the decision-making process that impacts their children's
education.

actions.title:
Parent Education
Childcare and
Resources
actions.description:
Childcare and parent resources will be available during parent education
classes when in-person offerings are provided and allowable. This action
provides low-income families support with free resources and childcare to
reduce any obstacles they may have for attending parent engagement
events. The goal of this action is to meet the desired outcome in the self-
reflection tool on the CA School Dashboard (Over 95% of parents will
report high levels of satisfaction with school communication and
engagement opportunities on the annual parent survey).

actions.title:
Homeless/Foster
Youth Liaison
actions.description:
A Homeless/Foster Youth Liaison provides support to all District families in
the Magnolia School District Project HERO (Homeless Education
Renewing Opportunities) office which also serves approximately 0.5%
Foster Youth . This office provides a range of services including securing
food, clothing, transportation, housing assistance, health services,
extended learning programs, and financial literacy classes. This service is
supported by the research that informs us that when school districts
partner with mental health services, our Foster Youth demonstrate higher
levels of academic and social-emotional success in schools. The goal of
this action is to continue to decrease the number of Foster Youth being
suspended from school and continue to decrease the percentage of Foster
Youth who are chronically absent from school. Foster Youth made a 4.7%
decrease in Chronic Absenteeism and a 4.4% decrease in Suspension
Rate when you compare the 2023 and 2024 Dashboard results. This
service has shown to be an effective support for Foster Youth by
increasing their participation and attendance in school.

actions.title: Health Clerk Services
actions.description:
A l l s chools receive at least 7 hours of Health Clerk services to support
student health and follow-up on attendance related issues. This action is
principally directed to low-income and Foster Youth students and families
to support with health services they may not have access to on a regular
basis. Magnolia School District has 80.5% of families experiencing poverty
and 0.5% are Foster Youth. This service provides a consistent school staff
member making connections with students and families that results in a
significant impact on the increase of student attendance and participation
in school. Magnolia School District was recognized with the 2022
California Pivotal Practice (CAPP) Award for the impact the Student and
Attendance Engagement Teams made on student attendance. This team
includes all Health Clerks and they continue to be an integral part of this
team and support students and families on a daily basis with health and
attendance related concerns. The goal of this action is to continue to
decrease the percentage of chronically absent low-income students and
Foster Youth. Low-income students made a 8.5% decrease in Chronic
Absenteeism and Foster Youth made a 4.7% decrease in Chronic
Absenteeism when you compare the 2023 and 2024 Dashboard results.
This service has shown to be an effective support for low-income students
Foster Youth by increasing their participation and attendance in school by
supporting them with their health and attendance-related concerns.

actions.title:
Social
Worker/Counseling
Services
actions.description:
A District team of nine social workers will provide mental health services to
all nine elementary schools and counseling services will be provided based
on student and family need. This action is principally directed to low-
income and Foster Youth based on research that shows these two
subgroup of students do not always have immediate access to necessary
mental health services. The California Department of Social Services
references significant statistics specifically that 80% of Foster Youth have
significant mental health challenges as compared to 18-22% of the general
population. They also reference the American Academy of Pediatrics who
identified mental and behavioral health as the greatest unmet health need
for Foster Youth (DHCS, 2022). The American Psychological Association
in their May 2024 article, Mental Health Effects of Poverty, Hunger, and
Homelessness on Children and Teens, states that school-age children who
experience severe hunger are at increased risk for poor mental health and
lower academic performance, and often fall behind their peers in social and
emotional skills (apa.org). Magnolia School District has 80.5% of families
experiencing poverty and 0.5% are Foster Youth. The goal of this action is
to decrease the number of low-income and Foster Youth being suspended
from school and decrease the percentage of chronically absent students.
This would result in the increase of supports and services to ensure low-
income students and Foster Youth do not miss academic, behavioral and
social-emotional learning due to a suspension or absence from school.
This service provides mental health professionals onsite making these
connections with students and families resulting in a significant impact on
higher student attendance and lower suspension rates. Magnolia School
District was recognized with the 2022 California Pivotal Practice (CAPP)
Award for the impact the Student and Attendance Engagement Teams
made on student attendance. This team includes all Social Workers and
they continue to be an integral part of this team and support students and
families on a daily basis with mental health services. The goal of this action
is to continue to decrease the suspension rate and the percentage of
chronically absent low-income students and Foster Youth. Low-income
students maintained their suspension rate with a 0.2% slight decrease in
students suspended at least one day and Foster Youth made a 4.4%
decrease in in their suspension rate when you compare the 2023 and 2024
Dashboard results. Low-income students made a 8.5% decrease in
Chronic Absenteeism and Foster Youth made a 4.7% decrease in Chronic
Absenteeism when you compare the 2023 and 2024 Dashboard results.
This service has shown to be an effective support for low-income students
Foster Youth by increasing their participation and attendance in school by
supporting them with mental health services.

actions.title:
Behavior
Interventionist
Services
actions.description:
A District team of Behavior Interventionists are assigned based upon
student and program needs at all nine elementary schools. This action is
principally directed to low-income and Foster Youth based on research that
shows these two subgroup of students do not always have immediate
access to necessary mental health services. The California Department of
Social Services references significant statistics specifically that 80% of
Foster Youth have significant mental health challenges as compared to 18-
22% of the general population. They also reference the American
Academy of Pediatrics who identified mental and behavioral health as the
greatest unmet health need for Foster Youth (DHCS, 2022). The American
Psychological Association in their May 2024 article, Mental Health Effects
of Poverty, Hunger, and Homelessness on Children and Teens, states that
school-age children who experience severe hunger are at increased risk
for poor mental health and lower academic performance, and often fall
behind their peers in social and emotional skills (apa.org). Magnolia
School District has 80.5% of families experiencing poverty and 0.5% are
Foster Youth. The goal of this action is to decrease the number of low-
income and Foster Youth being suspended from school and decrease the
percentage of chronically absent students. This would result in the
increase of supports and services to ensure low-income students and
Foster Youth do not miss academic, behavioral and social-emotional
learning due to a suspension or absence from school. This service
provides mental health professionals onsite making these connections with
students and families resulting in a significant impact on higher student
attendance and lower suspension rates. Magnolia School District was
recognized with the 2022 California Pivotal Practice (CAPP) Award for the
impact the Student and Attendance Engagement Teams made on student
attendance. This team includes all Behavior Interventionists and they
continue to be an integral part of this team and support students and
families on a daily basis with mental health services. The goal of this action
is to continue to decrease the suspension rate and the percentage of
chronically absent low-income students and Foster Youth. Low-income
students maintained their suspension rate with a 0.2% slight decrease in
students suspended at least one day and Foster Youth made a 4.4%
decrease in in their suspension rate when you compare the 2023 and 2024
Dashboard results. Low-income students made a 8.5% decrease in
Chronic Absenteeism and Foster Youth made a 4.7% decrease in Chronic
Absenteeism when you compare the 2023 and 2024 Dashboard results.
Although there are decreases in the chronic absenteeism indicator, 22.4%
Foster Youth and 20% of Low-income students are still chronically absent.
This service has shown to be an effective support for low-income students
Foster Youth by increasing their participation and attendance in school by
supporting them with mental health services.

actions.title:
Attendance Incentive
Programs and
Partnerships
actions.description:
All schools receive a $1,000 allocation for various attendance incentive
programs, including communications, awareness, and recognition
programs. The California Department of Education (cde.ca.gov) provides
a list of School Attendance Improvement Strategies and item #14 states:
Hold a drawing for special prizes for individual students or classes that
show improved attendance. This action includes the collaboration with the
Gang Reduction and Intervention Partnership (GRIP) to provide students
with educational and social-emotional learning opportunities to address
attendance, academics, and behavior. In addition, the GRIP program will
provide additional support to address chronic absenteeism. The Learning
Policy Institute's report on Reducing Chronic Absenteeism (August 2024)
states that partnerships with external organizations are critical to schools
and their ability to foster student well-being and achievement through
integrated systems of support. These partnerships work to support school
and district goals. GRIP aims to work in tandem with the schools on
reducing chronic absenteeism. This action is principally directed to low-
income and Foster Youth students and families to support with attendance-
related issues and the necessary supports and services students may not
have access to on a regular basis. Magnolia School District has 80.5% of
families experiencing poverty and 0.5% are Foster Youth. This service
provides incentives and partnerships that result in a significant impact on
the increase of student attendance and participation in school. The goal of
this action is to continue to decrease the percentage of chronically absent
low-income students and Foster Youth. Low-income students made a
8.5% decrease in Chronic Absenteeism and Foster Youth made a 4.7%
decrease in Chronic Absenteeism when you compare the 2023 and 2024
Dashboard results. These services have shown to be an effective support
for low-income students Foster Youth by increasing their participation and
attendance in school by supporting them with their attendance-related
concerns.

actions.title:
School Positive
Behavioral
Interventions and
Supports (PBIS)
actions.description:
All schools receive a $3,000 allocation for various Positive Behavioral
Interventions and Supports (PBIS) programs, including communications,
program monitoring tools, specific intervention resources and materials,
PBIS committee meetings and trainings, and recognition programs. This
action is principally directed to low-income and Foster Youth based on
research that shows these two subgroup of students do not always have
immediate access to necessary mental health services and positive
discipline activities. The California Department of Social Services
references significant statistics specifically that 80% of Foster Youth have
significant mental health challenges as compared to 18-22% of the general
population. They also reference the American Academy of Pediatrics who
identified mental and behavioral health as the greatest unmet health need
for Foster Youth (DHCS, 2022). The American Psychological Association
in their May 2024 article, Mental Health Effects of Poverty, Hunger, and
Homelessness on Children and Teens, states that school-age children who
experience severe hunger are at increased risk for poor mental health and
lower academic performance, and often fall behind their peers in social and
emotional skills (apa.org). Magnolia School District has 80.5% of families
experiencing poverty and 0.5% are Foster Youth. The goal of this action is
to decrease the number of low-income and Foster Youth being suspended
from school and decrease the percentage of chronically absent students.
This would result in the increase of supports and services to ensure low-
income students and Foster Youth do not miss academic, behavioral and
social-emotional learning due to a suspension or absence from school.
This service provides various PBIS programs resulting in a significant
impact on higher student participation and attendance in school and lower
suspension rates. The goal of this action is to continue to decrease the
Suspension Rate and the percentage of chronically absent low-income
students and Foster Youth. Low-income students maintained their
suspension rate with a 0.2% slight decrease in students suspended at
least one day and Foster Youth made a 4.4% decrease in in their
suspension rate when you compare the 2023 and 2024 Dashboard results.
Low-income students made a 8.5% decrease in Chronic Absenteeism and
Foster Youth made a 4.7% decrease in Chronic Absenteeism when you
compare the 2023 and 2024 Dashboard results. Although there are
decreases in the chronic absenteeism indicator, 22.4% Foster Youth and
20% of Low-income students are still chronically absent. This service has
shown to be an effective support for low-income students Foster Youth by
increasing their participation and attendance in school by supporting them
with mental health services and programs.

actions.title:
Social-Emotional
Learning (SEL)/Anti-
Bullying Programs
and Resources
Partially funded with
Learning Recovery
Emergency Block
Grant (LREBG)
Funds
(Amount
$502,000.00)
actions.description:
All schools will receive District support and resources for implementation of
site-level social-emotional learning (SEL) program implementation and
anti-bullying education. This action is principally directed to low-income
and Foster Youth based on research that shows these two subgroup of
students tend to display significantly higher needs in the areas of social-
emotional and social-behavioral well-being and do not always have
immediate access to necessary mental health services and positive
discipline activities. The American Psychological Association in their May
2024 article, Mental Health Effects of Poverty, Hunger, and Homelessness
on Children and Teens, states that school-age children who experience
severe hunger are at increased risk for poor mental health and lower
academic performance, and often fall behind their peers in social and
emotional skills (apa.org). Magnolia School District has 80.5% of families
experiencing poverty and 0.5% are Foster Youth. The goal of this action is
to decrease the number of low-income and Foster Youth being suspended
from school and decrease the percentage of chronically absent students.
This would result in the increase of supports and services to ensure low-
income students and Foster Youth do not miss academic, behavioral and
social-emotional learning due to a suspension or absence from school.
This action would provide access to SEL curriculum materials (Second
Step) and supplemental resources (Calm Classroom) to encourage
positive discipline, attendance, and participation in school. The goal of this
action is to continue to decrease the Suspension Rate and the percentage
of chronically absent low-income students and Foster Youth. Low-income
students maintained their suspension rate with a 0.2% slight decrease in
students suspended at least one day and Foster Youth made a 4.4%
decrease in in their suspension rate when you compare the 2023 and 2024
Dashboard results. Low-income students made a 8.5% decrease in
Chronic Absenteeism and Foster Youth made a 4.7% decrease in Chronic
Absenteeism when you compare the 2023 and 2024 Dashboard results.
This service has shown to be an effective support for low-income students
Foster Youth by increasing their participation and attendance in school by
supporting them with mental health services and programs.
Through Learning Recovery Emergency Block Grant (LREBG) Funds, all
schools will receive a structured SEL-infused program that includes
physical activities and games in a rotation schedule where students
practice mindfulness and growth mindset exercises with embedded social-
emotional learning lessons based the Collaborative for Academic, Social,
and Emotional Learning (CASEL) competencies to support students and to
address other barriers to learning. This action would be monitored and
measured by the Chronic Absenteeism Indicator, Attendance Rates,
Suspension Rate Indicator, Expulsion Records, and Student Climate
Survey data.
Based on the Magnolia School District Needs Assessment, all nine
elementary schools received a "Very High" or "High" status level on the
Chronic Absenteeism Indicator. It is important to note that although all
nine elementary schools received these status levels, all nine schools
declined significantly in their chronic absenteeism percentage as compared
to the previous year.
Below is a summary of each school's significant decline in their percentage
of chronically absent students:
Baden-Powell School: 28.7% to 23.4% students chronically absent;
Disney School: 18.1% to 13% students chronically absent;
Juliette Low School of the Arts: 35.5% to 20.2% students chronically
absent;
Marshall School: 20.3% to 16.6% students chronically absent;
Maxwell School: 29.7% to 19.3% students chronically absent;
Robert M. Pyles STEM Academy: 29.5% to 20.3% students chronically
absent;
Salk School: 29.3% to 20.2% students chronically absent;
Dr. Albert Schweitzer Leadership Academy: 23.7% to 15% students
chronically absent;
Walter School: 25.9% to 20.8% students chronically absent.
The site-level structured supplemental SEL program with physical activities
and games has made a significant impact on student engagement and
attendance. The data is clear and demonstrates the impact of this service
and how it positively affects students. The District will continue to fund this
action with LREBG funds and continue the trajectory of declining in the
percentage of chronic absenteeism across the District. The funded
program has evidence-based social-emotional strategies from the
Collaborative for Academic, Social, and Emotional Learning (CASEL)
Framework that helps cultivate skills and environments that advance
students' learning and development. The benefits of social and emotional
learning (SEL) are well-researched with evidence showing that when SEL
is embedded in the classroom, it produces positive outcomes for students
(casel.org/fundementals-of-sel).

goals.description:
Goal 2: Pupil Outcomes
ALL Magnolia School District students will be provided with quality instructional programs and
services to become lifelong learners, and will show continuous progress in academic and language
proficiency in order to meet or exceed grade level standards.

actions.title:
Professional
Development
actions.description:
Comprehensive professional development for teachers that is aligned to
research-based and high-quality curriculum, Common Core State
Standards, instructional strategies, and assessment practices. Induction
for beginning teachers and PAR for experienced teachers will be provided
to support program coherence across all content areas. Professional
development content will be supported by Educational Services staff and
include programs, services, and strategies specifically designed to meet
the needs of English Learners, students with disabilities, students with
exceptional needs, low-income students, and Foster Youth. Data
continues to show an achievement gap in ELA based on the state
assessment (SBAC): Overall: 46.65% of students met or exceeded
standard; English Learners: 18.02% of students met or exceeded standard;
SocioEconomically Disadvantaged: 43.32% of students met or exceeded
standard; Foster Youth: 21.05% met or exceeded standard; and Students
with Disabilities: 18.69% met or exceeded standard.
Data continues to show an achievement gap in Math based on the state
assessment (SBAC): Overall: 40.53% of students met or exceeded
standard; English Learners: 19.45% of students met or exceeded standard;
SocioEconomically Disadvantaged: 37.09% of students met or exceeded
standard; Foster Youth: 21.05% met or exceeded standard; and Students
with Disabilities: 17.16% met or exceeded standard.
Based on the 2024 Dashboard, the following subgroups and Schools
received a Red Performance Level on specific State Indicators:
District:
Students with Disabilities- Academic Indicator (ELA)
82 points below standard;
Long-Term English Learner - Academic Indicator (Math)
103 points below standard;
Dr. Albert Schweitzer Leadership Academy:
Students with Disabilities Academic Indicator (ELA)
91.5 points below standard;
Students with Disabilities Academic Indicator (Math)
105.2 points below standard;
Marshall School:
English Learner - English Learner Progress Indicator
37.3% making progress towards English proficiency;
Walter School:
Students with Disabilities Academic Indicator (ELA)
133.2 points below standard;
Students with Disabilities Academic Indicator (Math)
141.6 points below standard;
Juliette Low School of the Arts:
English Learners - English Learner Progress Indicator
39.4% making progress towards English proficiency;
Baden-Powell School:
Students with Disabilities Academic Indicator (ELA)
81.7 points below standard;
Maxwell School:
Students with Disabilities Academic Indicator (ELA) 96.8 points below
standard.
This action is principally directed to low-income, English Learners, and
Foster Youth students based on research that shows equitable access to
strong teachers is critical to closing achievement gaps. The Association
for Supervision and Curriculum Development (ASCD) highlights that
studies show well-prepared and well-supported teachers are important for
all students, but especially for students who come to school with greater
needs. The goal of this action is to meet and/or be on track to meet the
desired outcomes on all the Goal 2 metrics.

actions.title:
Instructional Support
Services:
Instructional
Practices Coaches
(IPCs), Student
Achievement
Teachers (SATs),
Science, Technology,
Engineering, Arts,
and Mathematics
(STEAM) Teachers
Partially funded with
Learning Recovery
Emergency Block
Grant (LREBG)
Funds
(Amount
$485,000.00)
actions.description:
Every school will have an Instructional Practices Coach (IPC) to provide
ongoing job-embedded coaching, professional development, and
coordination of targeted student intervention programs. IPCs will facilitate
Data Dialogue Days four times a year with each grade level at their school.
Targeted intervention programs and instructional coaching on core
academic programs, implementation support, and best practice strategies
specifically designed to meet the needs of English Learners, students with
disabilities, students with exceptional needs, low-income students, and
Foster Youth. Three Science, Technology, Engineering, Arts, and
Mathematics (STEAM) Teachers will enhance the academic program to
include high-interest, engagning, and targeted lessons for all students.
This action is principally directed to low-income students, English Learners,
and Foster youth. Data continues to show an achievement gap in ELA
based on the state assessment (SBAC): Overall: 46.65% of students met
or exceeded standard; English Learners: 18.02% of students met or
exceeded standard; SocioEconomically Disadvantaged: 43.32% of
students met or exceeded standard; Foster Youth: 21.05% met or
exceeded standard; and Students with Disabilities: 18.69% met or
exceeded standard.
Data continues to show an achievement gap in Math based on the state
assessment (SBAC): Overall: 40.53% of students met or exceeded
standard; English Learners: 19.45% of students met or exceeded standard;
SocioEconomically Disadvantaged: 37.09% of students met or exceeded
standard; Foster Youth: 21.05% met or exceeded standard; and Students
with Disabilities: 17.16% met or exceeded standard.
Based on the 2024 Dashboard, the following subgroups and Schools
received a Red Performance Level on specific State Indicators and a
specific action will be implemented to address how the District and school
sites will increase student achievement:
Districtwide Students with Disabilities scored 82 points below standard and
received a Red Performance Level on the Academic Indicator (ELA) and
Long-Term English Learners scored 103 points below standard and
received a Red Performance Level on the Academic Indicator (Math).
Each IPC will collaborate with each grade level at each of their schools to
ensure discussions and planning during Data Dialogues include targeted
interventions for students with disabilities and Long-Term English Learners
who are not performing at grade level.
Dr. Albert Schweitzer Leadership Academy Students with Disabilities
scored 91.5 points below standard and received a Red Performance Level
on the Academic Indicator (ELA) and scored 105.2 points below standard
and received a Red Performance Level on the Academic Indicator (Math).
The IPC will collaborate with each grade level to ensure discussions and
planning during Data Dialogues include support for teachers with strategies
to differentiate instruction and targeted interventions for students with
disabilities who are not performing at grade level.
Marshall School English Learners are making 37.3% progress towards
English proficiency and received a Red Performance Level on the English
Learner Progress Indicator. The IPC and SAT will collaborate with each
grade level to ensure discussions and planning during Data Dialogues
include support for teachers with strategies to differentiate instruction and
targeted interventions for English Learners who are not performing at
grade level.
Walter School Students with Disabilities scored 133.2 points below
standard and received a Red Performance Level on the Academic
Indicator (ELA) and scored 141.6 points below standard and received a
Red Performance Level on the Academic Indicator (Math). The IPC will
collaborate with each grade level to ensure discussions and planning
during Data Dialogues include support for teachers with strategies to
differentiate instruction and targeted interventions for students with
disabilities who are not performing at grade level.
Juliette Low School of the Arts English Learners are making 39.4%
progress towards English proficiency and received a Red Performance
Level on the English Learner Progress Indicator. The IPC will collaborate
with each grade level to ensure discussions and planning during Data
Dialogues include support for teachers with strategies to differentiate
instruction and targeted interventions for English Learners who are not
performing at grade level.
Baden-Powell School Students with Disabilities scored 81.7 points below
standard and received a Red Performance Level on the Academic
Indicator (ELA). The IPC will collaborate with each grade level to ensure
discussions and planning during Data Dialogues include support for
teachers with strategies to differentiate instruction and targeted
interventions for students with disabilities who are not performing at grade
level.
Maxwell School Students with Disabilities scored 96.8 points below
standard and received a Red Performance Level on the Academic
Indicator (ELA). The IPC will collaborate with each grade level to ensure
discussions and planning during Data Dialogues include support for
teachers with strategies to differentiate instruction and targeted
interventions for students with disabilities who are not performing at grade
level.
Through Learning Recovery Emergency Block Grant (LREBG) Funds,
three Student Achievement Teachers (SATs) will support professional
development and coaching in the areas of English Language Arts and
Math and other school specific needs. This action is supported by the
results of the LREBG Needs Assessment that demonstrates a continued
need to support professional growth and coaching in the core areas of
instruction. Most schools show progress in meeting the Distance From
Met targets on the state assessments in both English Language Arts and
Math. Targeted support for the schools with a slight decline in the
Distance From Met target will be provided through professional
development and coaching by Instructional Support Services. A meta-
analysis of 60 instructional coaching evaluations by Kraft, Blazar, & Hogan
(2018) found large, positive effects of coaching on instructional practice
(0.49 standard deviations). Instructional coaches, such as Student
Achievement Teachers (SATs), can provide personalized support to
teachers in the areas they need the most support to increase high-quality
education. In another study, The Impact of Instructional Coaching on
Teacher Competency, Job Satisfaction, and Student Growth, by Rebecca
A. Frazier (2018) the data demonstrated strong evidence that coached
teachers grew at higher rates in many aspects of their teaching practice
when compared to non-coached teachers.

actions.title:
Programs and
Assessment
Personnel
actions.description:
A District Programs and Assessment Team will provide ongoing support for
administration and analysis of student assessments and related data
reporting. Disaggregated student performance reports will be shared and
analyzed with school-level teams in order to identify needs of student
subgroups. Assessment personnel will prioritize supporting schools with
data and reports to ensure school-level teams have the most updated
information to support and design services for students who are not
meeting grade level expectations. This action is principally directed to low-
income students and English Learners. Data continues to show an
achievement gap in ELA based on the state assessment (SBAC): Overall:
46.65% of students met or exceeded standard; English Learners: 18.02%
of students met or exceeded standard and SocioEconomically
Disadvantaged: 43.32% of students met or exceeded standard. In Math
based on the state assessment (SBAC): Overall: 40.53% of students met
or exceeded standard; English Learners: 19.45% of students met or
exceeded standard and SocioEconomically Disadvantaged: 37.09% of
students met or exceeded standard. The research shows having a better
understanding of the educational experiences of students with greater
needs is necessary to improve services and support to increase student
achievement. In an EdSurge data analytics article titled, How Data Drives
Strategies for Improved Student Outcomes (April 2024) they state that
data-driven decisions are increasingly recognized as a critical component
of K-12 education, enhancing personalized learning, improving
assessment and feedback, optimizing resource allocation and fostering
early intervention. They continue to state that various types of analysis
helps educators make informed choices that directly affect student learning
and school effectiveness. The goal of this action is to meet the desired
outcomes on the Academic State indicator on the CA School Dashboard
as reflected in the metric outcomes for this goal.

actions.title:
Student
Assessments and
Data Management
System
actions.description:
A comprehensive platform for student assessments and data management
will be purchased each year to ensure that student performance data is
adequately collected, reported, and analyzed on a regular basis. Using an
additional feature, Educlimber, data will be collected and disaggregated to
monitor growth of unduplicated student groups, students with disabilities,
and students with exceptional needs. This action is principally directed to
low-income and English Learner students based on research that shows
having a better understanding of the educational experiences of students
with greater needs is necessary to improve services and support to
increase student achievement. Data continues to show an achievement
gap in ELA based on the state assessment (SBAC): Overall: 46.65% of
students met or exceeded standard; English Learners: 18.02% of students
met or exceeded standard and SocioEconomically Disadvantaged: 43.32%
of students met or exceeded standard. In Math based on the state
assessment (SBAC): Overall: 40.53% of students met or exceeded
standard; English Learners: 19.45% of students met or exceeded standard
and SocioEconomically Disadvantaged: 37.09% of students met or
exceeded standard. The research shows having a better understanding of
the educational experiences of students with greater needs is necessary to
improve services and support to increase student achievement. In an
EdSurge data analytics article titled, How Data Drives Strategies for
Improved Student Outcomes (April 2024) they state that data-driven
decisions are increasingly recognized as a critical component of K-12
education, enhancing personalized learning, improving assessment and
feedback, optimizing resource allocation and fostering early intervention.
They continue to state that various types of analysis helps educators make
informed choices that directly affect student learning and school
effectiveness. The goal of this action is to meet the desired outcomes on
the Academic State indicator on the CA School Dashboard as reflected in
the metric outcomes for this goal.

actions.title: Music Teachers
actions.description:
Seven (7) music teachers will provide music instruction to all students in
grades TK - 6th grade. This service will provide all students with music
instruction throughout the school year. Magnolia School District has
80.5% of families experiencing poverty based on students who qualify for a
free or reduced priced meal. Embedding music classes during the
instructional day as an enrichment opportunity provides access to low-
income students and supports higher student achievement on the
Academic Indicator. This action is principally directed to low-income
students based on the research that students of poverty often do not have
the resources to participate in extracurricular activities such as music
classes to learn how to play an instrument. The U.S. Department of
Education posted an article by the Arts Education Partnership, "Music
Matters: How Music Education Helps Students Learn, Achieve, and
Succeed." (2011). The article states the benefits of music include how
music education facilitates student academic achievement by improving
recall and retention of verbal information; advances math achievement;
boosts reading and English Language Arts skills; and improves state
assessment scores. The article continues to describe how music
education prepares students to learn by helping to develop their basic
mental skills and capacities. Music education prepares the brain for
achievement, fosters superior working memory and cultivates better
thinking skills.
A second Magnolia School District (MSD) Band Program will continue to
be implemented to provide low-income students more access to
instruments and provide them the opportunity to gain skills they can utilize
when attending middle and high school. The MSD Choir Program will
continue on it's second year of implementation to support low-income
students and provide them the exposure to more Visual and Performing
Arts (VAPA) experiences they can utilize when attending middle and high
school. The goal of this action is to meet the desired outcomes on the
Academic State indicator on the CA School Dashboard as reflected in the
metric outcomes for this goal - which states all socio-economically
disadvantaged students will increase Performance Level to Green or
higher on the Dashboard.

actions.title:
Arts Education
Programs
actions.description:
Arts instructional supplies, materials, programs, and services will be
provided to all students by grade level across the four Arts domains of
visual art, theater, music, and dance. Magnolia School District has 80.5%
of families experiencing poverty based on students who qualify for a free or
reduced priced meal. Embedding music classes during the instructional
day as an enrichment opportunity provides access to low-income students
and supports higher student achievement on the Academic Indicator. This
action is principally directed to low-income students based on the research
that students of poverty often do not have the resources to participate in
extracurricular activities such as music classes to learn how to play an
instrument. The U.S. Department of Education posted an article by the
Arts Education Partnership, "Music Matters: How Music Education Helps
Students Learn, Achieve, and Succeed." (2011). The article states the
benefits of music include how music education facilitates student academic
achievement by improving recall and retention of verbal information;
advances math achievement; boosts reading and English Language Arts
skills; and improves state assessment scores. The article continues to
describe how music education prepares students to learn by helping to
develop their basic mental skills and capacities. Music education prepares
the brain for achievement, fosters superior working memory and cultivates
better thinking skills.
A second Magnolia School District (MSD) Band Program will continue to
be implemented to provide low-income students more access to
instruments and provide them the opportunity to gain skills they can utilize
when attending middle and high school. The MSD Choir Program will
continue on it's second year of implementation to support low-income
students and provide them the exposure to more Visual and Performing
Arts (VAPA) experiences they can utilize when attending middle and high
school. The goal of this action is to meet the desired outcomes on the
Academic State indicator on the CA School Dashboard as reflected in the
metric outcomes for this goal - which states all socio-economically
disadvantaged students will increase Performance Level to Green or
higher on the Dashboard.

actions.title:
Sixth Grade Outdoor
Science School
actions.description:
Outdoor Science Camp costs will be covered for all sixth grade students in
order to provide them with access to this educational enrichment
opportunity. This action is principally directed to low-income students and
English Learners based on the research that students of poverty and
second-language learners often do not have the monetary resources to
participate in science camps. Magnolia School District has 80.5% of
families experiencing poverty based on students who qualify for a free or
reduced priced meal and 40.9% are classified as English Learners.
Providing this opportunity for low-income students provides access to an
outdoor science experience they may not be able to afford outside of
school. Baseline 2024 Dashboard data demonstrates that low-income
students are benefiting from this service but there is an achievement gap
when comparing English Learners with the Overall data. Overall 29.76%
met or exceeded standard on the California Science Test as compared to
26.27% of socioeconomically disadvantaged students met or exceeded
standard. Approximately 5.05% of English Learners met or exceeded
standard on the CAST which is a gap of 24.71% from the Overall score.
The goal of this action is to meet the desired outcomes on the Academic
State indicator on the CA School Dashboard and California Science Test
(CAST) as reflected in the metric outcomes for this goal.

actions.title: Library Services
actions.description:
All schools will receive library services to ensure student access to high-
quality reading materials at school and at home. Each school will have a
Library Media Clerk to provide library access to all TK-6 grade students.
This action is principally directed to low-income students who do not
always have the immediate literacy resources and supports to help them
with their academic and social reading needs. Magnolia School District has
80.5% of families experiencing poverty based on students who qualify for a
free or reduced priced meal. The American Association of School
Librarians (AASL) did a study on the positive impacts school libraries have
on students of poverty. The research indicates that adequate school
libraries and library staff are essential to leveling the playing field for
students of poverty and the resources provided are essential to helping
low-income students increase achievement. Data shows a slight
achievement gap in ELA based on the state assessment (SBAC): Overall:
46.65% of students met or exceeded standard and 43.32% of low-income
students met or exceeded standard. The goal of this action is to meet the
desired outcomes on the Academic State indicator on the CA School
Dashboard as reflected in the metric outcomes for this goal.

actions.title:
College and Career
Readiness (CCR)
Programs
actions.description:
Every school will receive $1000 for College and Career Readiness
resources and activities, including student success skills materials (student
planners and binders), career exploration programs, college awareness
events, partnership programs with local colleges, and other opportunities
as available. This action is principally directed to low-income, who often
lack the guidance and support necessary to have college and career
mindsets. Magnolia School District has 80.5% of families experiencing
poverty based on students who qualify for a free or reduced priced meal.
The National Center for Education Statistics reported that low-income
students do not always possess a good foundation of education ability and
often do not have the organizational skills to prepare their long-term
college and career goals. The goal of this action is to meet the desired
outcomes on the Academic State indicator on the CA School Dashboard
as reflected in the metric outcomes for this goal.

actions.title:
School Multi-Tiered
System of
Supports(MTSS)/Dat
a Dialogue Days
actions.description:
Four Multi-Tiered System of Supports(MTSS)/Data Dialogue Days will be
provided to each classroom teacher to analyze student performance data
and engage in collaborative team planning for instruction and interventions.
Planning focus areas will include programs, services, and strategies
specifically designed to meet the needs of English Learners, students with
disabilities, students with exceptional needs, low-income students, and
Foster Youth.
This action is principally directed to low-income students, English Learners,
and Foster youth. Data continues to show an achievement gap in ELA
based on the state assessment (SBAC): Overall: 46.65% of students met
or exceeded standard; English Learners: 18.02% of students met or
exceeded standard; SocioEconomically Disadvantaged: 43.32% of
students met or exceeded standard; Foster Youth: 21.05% met or
exceeded standard; and Students with Disabilities: 18.69% met or
exceeded standard.
Data continues to show an achievement gap in Math based on the state
assessment (SBAC): Overall: 40.53% of students met or exceeded
standard; English Learners: 19.45% of students met or exceeded standard;
SocioEconomically Disadvantaged: 37.09% of students met or exceeded
standard; Foster Youth: 21.05% met or exceeded standard; and Students
with Disabilities: 17.16% met or exceeded standard.
Based on the 2024 Dashboard, the following subgroups and Schools
received a Red Performance Level on specific State Indicators and a
specific action will be implemented to address how the District and school
sites will increase student achievement:
Districtwide Students with Disabilities scored 82 points below standard and
received a Red Performance Level on the Academic Indicator (ELA) and
Long-Term English Learners scored 103 points below standard and
received a Red Performance Level on the Academic Indicator (Math).
Each IPC will collaborate with each grade level at each of their schools to
ensure discussions and planning during Data Dialogues include targeted
interventions for students with disabilities and Long-Term English Learners
who are not performing at grade level.
Dr. Albert Schweitzer Leadership Academy Students with Disabilities
scored 91.5 points below standard and received a Red Performance Level
on the Academic Indicator (ELA) and scored 105.2 points below standard
and received a Red Performance Level on the Academic Indicator (Math).
The IPC will collaborate with each grade level to ensure discussions and
planning during Data Dialogues include support for teachers with strategies
to differentiate instruction and targeted interventions for students with
disabilities who are not performing at grade level.
Marshall School English Learners are making 37.3% progress towards
English proficiency and received a Red Performance Level on the English
Learner Progress Indicator. The IPC and SAT will collaborate with each
grade level to ensure discussions and planning during Data Dialogues
include support for teachers with strategies to differentiate instruction and
targeted interventions for English Learners who are not performing at
grade level.
Walter School Students with Disabilities scored 133.2 points below
standard and received a Red Performance Level on the Academic
Indicator (ELA) and scored 141.6 points below standard and received a
Red Performance Level on the Academic Indicator (Math). The IPC will
collaborate with each grade level to ensure discussions and planning
during Data Dialogues include support for teachers with strategies to
differentiate instruction and targeted interventions for students with
disabilities who are not performing at grade level.
Juliette Low School of the Arts English Learners are making
39.4%progress towards English proficiency and received a Red
Performance Level on the English Learner Progress Indicator. The IPC
will collaborate with each grade level to ensure discussions and planning
during Data Dialogues include support for teachers with strategies to
differentiate instruction and targeted interventions for English Learners who
are not performing at grade level.
Baden-Powell School Students with Disabilities scored 81.7 points below

Indicator (ELA). The IPC will collaborate with each grade level to ensure
discussions and planning during Data Dialogues include support for
teachers with strategies to differentiate instruction and targeted
interventions for students with disabilities who are not performing at grade
level.
Maxwell School Students with Disabilities scored 96.8 points below
standard and received a Red Performance Level on the Academic
Indicator (ELA). The IPC will collaborate with each grade level to ensure
discussions and planning during Data Dialogues include support for
teachers with strategies to differentiate instruction and targeted
interventions for students with disabilities who are not performing at grade

actions.title:
After-School
Intervention
Programs
actions.description:
Expanded Learning Enrichment Afterschool Clubs are offered at all nine
schools and are conducted by Magnolia School District teachers. These
clubs are designed to enrich students in various areas such as academic
tutoring, drama club, dance club, musical theater, yoga, sign language,
robotics, cooking club, eSports, journalism, and many more clubs. This
action is principally directed to low-income, English learners, and Foster
Youth who do not always have the opportunities to participate in
enrichment type activities. This action provides extended learning
opportunities to assist in closing the achievement gap for these subgroups
of students. The goal of this action is to meet the desired outcomes on
the Academic State indicator on the CA School Dashboard as reflected in
the metric outcomes for this goal.

actions.title:
Special Education
Coordinator
actions.description:
Two District Special Education Coordinators will work with all schools to
ensure programs and services are well-designed to meet the needs of
Students with Disabilities and increase their parent engagement. The goal
of this action is to meet the desired outcomes on the Academic State
indicator on the CA School Dashboard as reflected in the metric outcomes
for this goal.

actions.title: Summer Academy
actions.description:
S ummer Academy will be provided to accelerate and extend learning for
English learners, low-income students, Foster Youth, Students with
Disabilities, and other at-risk students as needed. This action is principally
directed to low-income students, English learners, and Foster Youth to
prevent summer learning loss. Research shows that children experience
learning loss when they do not engage in educational activities during the
summer. This summer learning loss is cumulative and research has
shown low-income, English learners, and Foster Youth students to be
nearly three grade equivalents behind their more affluent peers in reading
by the end of the fifth grade. To monitor progress students will be given a
pre- and post assessment in reading and math.
Based on the 2024 Dashboard, the following subgroups and Schools
received a Red Performance Level on specific State Indicators and a
specific action will be implemented to address how the District and school
sites will increase student achievement:
Districtwide Students with Disabilities scored 82 points below standard and
received a Red Performance Level on the Academic Indicator (ELA) and
Long-Term English Learners scored 103 points below standard and
received a Red Performance Level on the Academic Indicator (Math).
Each IPC will collaborate with each grade level at each of their schools to
ensure discussions and planning during Data Dialogues include targeted
interventions for students with disabilities and Long-Term English Learners

Dr. Albert Schweitzer Leadership Academy Students with Disabilities

The IPC will collaborate with each grade level to ensure discussions and

collaborate with each grade level to ensure discussions and planning

differentiate instruction and targeted interventions for students with

will collaborate with each grade level to ensure discussions and planning

Baden-Powell School Students with Disabilities scored 81.7 points below

discussions and planning during Data Dialogues include support for
teachers with strategies to differentiate instruction and targeted

Maxwell School Students with Disabilities scored 96.8 points below

actions.title:
School-level Services
for English Learners
and Long-Term
English Learners:
Impact Teachers,
Supplemental
Programs, and
Districtwide
Intervention Materials
Partially funded with
Learning Recovery
Emergency Block
Grant (LREBG)
Funds
(Amount
$120,000.00)
actions.description:
All schools will receive an allocation based upon English Learner
enrollment to provide targeted programs and services administered by
Impact teachers and other support staff as outlined in each School Plan for
Student Achievement (SPSA) anchored in the English Language
Arts/English Language Development State Standards. Intervention
materials in reading and math will be utilized to provide supplemental
instruction to English Learners and Long-Term English Learner (LTEL)
students who are not meeting grade level expectations.
This action is principally directed to English Learners, LTEL, and students
at-risk of becoming LTEL to support their academic language
development. As a result of this data Impact Teachers will utilize English
Learner data provide targeted intervention to support the development of
English Language proficiency.
This action is principally directed to low-income students, English Learners,
and LTELs. Data continues to show an achievement gap in ELA based on
the state assessment (SBAC): Overall: 46.65% of students met or
exceeded standard; English Learners: 18.02% of students met or
exceeded standard; SocioEconomically Disadvantaged: 43.32% of
students met or exceeded standard; LTEL: 4.93% met or exceeded
standard. In Math based on the state assessment (SBAC): Overall:
40.53% of students met or exceeded standard; English Learners: 19.45%
of students met or exceeded standard; SocioEconomically Disadvantaged:
37.09% of students met or exceeded standard; LTEL: 6.17% met or
exceeded standard.
Based on the 2024 Dashboard, the following subgroups and schools
received a Red Performance Level on specific State Indicators and a
specific action will be implemented to address how the District and school
sites will increase student achievement:
Districtwide Long-Term English Learners scored 103 points below
standard and received a Red Performance Level on the Academic
Indicator (Math). Each IPC will collaborate with each grade level at each of
their schools to ensure discussions and planning during Data Dialogues
include targeted interventions for Long-Term English Learners who are not
at grade level.
Marshall School English Learners are making 37.3% progress towards
English proficiency and received a Red Performance Level on the English
Learner Progress Indicator. The IPC and SAT will collaborate with each
grade level to ensure discussions and planning during Data Dialogues
include support for teachers with strategies to differentiate instruction and
targeted interventions for English Learners who are not making progress
towards English proficiency.
Juliette Low School of the Arts English Learners are making 39.4%
progress towards English proficiency and received a Red Performance
Level on the English Learner Progress Indicator. The IPC will collaborate
with each grade level to ensure discussions and planning during Data
Dialogues include support for teachers with strategies to differentiate
instruction and targeted interventions for English Learners who are not
making progress towards English proficiency.
Through Learning Recovery Emergency Block Grant (LREBG) Funds,
intervention evidence-based learning supports and materials in English
Language Arts to accelerate student academic proficiency and English
language proficiency will be purchased for targeted intervention and small
group instruction. This action is supported by the results of the LREBG
Needs Assessment. In English Language Arts (ELA), four schools
received a "Low" status, four schools received a "Medium" status, and one
school received a "High" status. In the area of Math, six schools received
a "Medium" status and three schools received a "Low" status. No schools
received a "Very Low" status. This is significant because the Districtwide
instructional efforts are consistent and program coherence is a priority
across all schools. In both ELA and Math, half of the schools either
maintained or increased in the Distance From Met score. For the schools
with the greatest need in ELA, the supplemental intervention program from
the District's Core Reading program will be funded to support Targeted
Intervention. In the area of math, supplemental math supports and
intervention materials will be purchased with LREBG funds to support Math
Labs for second, third, and fourth grade students. Teachers at those grade
levels will participate in Math Labs with an Orange County Department of
Education Math Coach, Student Achievement Teacher (SAT) and their
Instructional Practices Coach (IPC). This action will fund the instructional
supplemental materials to support Targeted Intervention. The research is
very clear about intervention programs and their effectiveness. According
to John Hattie's research (2008) intervention programs had a statistically
significant impact (effect size) on student achievement. Response to
Intervention (RtI) has an effect size of 1.07 which means it is highly
effective. In other words, if a district is successfully implementing RtI, then
it is producing a 1.07 standard deviation greater impact on student
outcomes (higher test scores) than a district that is not implementing RtI
approaches or interventions.
The goal of this action is to meet the desired outcomes on the Academic
State indicator on the CA School Dashboard as reflected in the metric
outcomes for this goal.

goals.description:
Goal 3: Basic Services
Magnolia School District will provide optimal conditions for learning, including safe and clean
facilities, quality standards-aligned curriculum, access to technology for learning, and highly-
qualified teachers.

actions.title:
Standards-Aligned
Textbooks and
Materials
actions.description:
High-quality, standards-aligned instructional materials will be purchased
annually in alignment with State and local adoption criteria and timelines.
This includes the cost of annual consumables for all content areas.

actions.title:
Student Devices and
Peripherals/Network
actions.description:
Chromebooks and peripherals (chargers, headphones, mice, etc.) are
p u rchased for every student on a four-phase purchasing schedule. A high-
speed digital internet connection is maintained district-wide to ensure
continuous connectivity in all classrooms. This action is principally directed
to low-income students who tend to have limited access to high-quality
technology and digital literacy. Magnolia School District has 80.5% of
families experiencing poverty based on students who qualify for a free or
reduced priced meal. Research suggests that providing low-income
students with devices to access digital tools and innovation provides them
long-term skills they will need to achieve college and career goals. In the
article titled, How Technology is Leveling the Playing Field for Low-Income
Students (J.Rahimi, 2024) Rahimi states that digital platforms and tools
enable low-income students to engage with peers from around the world,
exchange ideas, solve problems, and work on projects together. He
continues to state that this connectivity fosters teamwork and helps
students develop essential communication and collaboration skills. The
desired outcome for this goal is to meet the Technology Maintenance
Records and Usage Reports metric established for this goal.

actions.title:
Classroom
Presentation
Technology and
Other Digital Tools
and Resources
actions.description:
Interactive whiteboards/LED screens and document cameras are updated
and serviced on a maintenance schedule to ensure that all classrooms
have equipment necessary for innovative lesson delivery. Ensuring that
technology is on a rotating cycle to be upgraded and updated allows for the
instructional staff to use the most updated version of technology without
any timing gaps. Instructional staff and students will have access to
interactive and innovative digital software, platforms and programs that will
enhance learning, lesson planning, assessments, and feedback. This
action is principally directed to low-income students to provide innovative
learning environments with the most up-to-date technology for a high
quality instruction and educational experience. Magnolia School District
has 80.5% of families experiencing poverty based on students who qualify
for a free or reduced priced meal. The integration of technology has a
direct impact on low-income students and increases student engagement,
access, and academic achievement. Research suggests that providing
low-income students with devices to access digital tools and innovation
provides them long-term skills they will need to achieve college and career
goals. In the article titled, How Technology is Leveling the Playing Field for
Low-Income Students (J.Rahimi, 2024) Rahimi states that digital platforms
and tools enable low-income students to engage with peers from around
the world, exchange ideas, solve problems, and work on projects together.
He continues to state that this connectivity fosters teamwork and helps
students develop essential communication and collaboration skills.The
desired outcome for this goal is to meet the Technology Maintenance
Records and Usage Reports metric established for this goal.

actions.title:
District Technology
Personnel/Infrastruct
ure
actions.description:
A District technology team provides ongoing support for students and
families through a help desk process and administration of hotspots and
infrastructure. Infrastructure costs include equipment and services
necessary for connectivity. A Science, Technology, Engineering, Arts,
Mathematics (STEAM) Technician will support activities related to STEAM.
This action is principally directed to low-income and Foster Youth students
who tend to not have access to immediate support with technology and
connectivity needs. The Casey Family Programs March (2024) article on
How Can the Child Welfare System Help Youth and Families Access
Needed Technology? stated Foster Youth and children of poverty often
face a digital divide and computers and internet access should be
considered a basic need for them. This action is principally directed to low-
income students and English Learners. Data continues to show an
achievement gap in ELA based on the state assessment (SBAC): Overall:
46.65% of students met or exceeded standard; English Learners: 18.02%
of students met or exceeded standard; SocioEconomically Disadvantaged:
43.32% of students met or exceeded standard. In Math based on the state
assessment (SBAC): Overall: 40.53% of students met or exceeded
standard; English Learners: 19.45% of students met or exceeded standard;
SocioEconomically Disadvantaged: 37.09% of students met or exceeded
standard. The desired outcome for this goal is to meet the Technology
Maintenance Records and Usage Reports metric established for this goal.

actions.title:
Campus and
Playground
Supervision;
Increased Access
and Safety on
Playgrounds
actions.description:
Staff will be provided for campus and playground supervision, including
Expanded Learning staff, to ensure student safety on campus before
school, after school, and during recess and lunch breaks. This action is
principally directed to low-income students and Foster Youth who may not
always feel connected to school and can benefit from additional staff that
can support them during recess and lunch breaks and before and after
school by implementing strategies that can help students feel connected
and ultimately feel successful in school. Research suggests that feeling
safe and connected supports student learning. The Learning Policy
Institute published a brief titled, "Safe Schools, Thriving Students: What
We Know About Creating Safe and Supportive Schools (DePaoli and
McCombs, 2023) that found when students feel welcome and connected to
their school communities, they have improved mental health, academic,
and behavioral outcomes and are less likely to engage in high-risk
behaviors. District student climate survey data indicates that 89% of
students report they feel comfortable going to a staff member at school
when they have a problem; 82% of students report that if they feel sad or
worried, they know who to go to for help. Magnolia School District has
80.5% of families experiencing poverty and 0.5% are Foster Youth. 22.4%
of Foster Youth and 20% of Low Income students were chronically absent
in comparison to 18.3% of all students who have been identified to be
chronically absent on the 2024 Dashboard. Although these two subgroups
have higher chronic absenteeism percentages than the District overall
percentage, it is important to note that both subgroups made a significant
decrease in their chronic absenteeism percentage when you compare their
2023 and 2024 Chronic Absenteeism Indicator on the Dashboard: Low-
income students made a 8.5% decrease and Foster Youth made a 4.7%
decrease. Based on the 2024 Dashboard, 0.9% of low-income students
were suspended at least one day and 1.8% of Foster Youth were
suspended at least one day. Both subgroups decreased in their
suspension percentages (low-income decreased 0.2% and Foster Youth
decreased 4.4%) demonstrating overall progress. The goal of this action is
to continue to decrease chronic absenteeism and suspension rates for low-
income and Foster Youth on the 2025 Dashboard.

actions.title:
Grade Span
Adjustment Staffing
actions.description:
Class sizes are based on state and federal laws and labor agreements.
T his action is principally directed to low-income students to provide
increased opportunities for small group and targeted instruction in the
areas of reading and math. This action is principally directed to low-income
students, English Learners, and Foster youth. Data continues to show an
achievement gap in ELA based on the state assessment (SBAC): Overall:
46.65% of students met or exceeded standard; English Learners: 18.02%
of students met or exceeded standard; SocioEconomically Disadvantaged:
43.32% of students met or exceeded standard; Foster Youth: 21.05% met
or exceeded standard.
Data continues to show an achievement gap in Math based on the state
assessment (SBAC): Overall: 40.53% of students met or exceeded
standard; English Learners: 19.45% of students met or exceeded standard;
SocioEconomically Disadvantaged: 37.09% of students met or exceeded
standard; Foster Youth: 21.05% met or exceeded standard.
The goal of this action is to meet the desired outcomes on the Academic
State indicator on the CA School Dashboard as reflected in the metric
outcomes for this goal.

actions.title:
Site Custodial and
Maintenance Staff
actions.description:
Additional site custodial and maintenance positions ensure adequate
cleaning beyond the school day for supplementary programs and services.
A study conducted by the Center for Facilities Research found a correlation
between the cleanliness of school facilities and students' academic
achievements. This action is principally directed to low-income students to
provide learning environments that support a positive and engaging
learning atmosphere. District student climate survey data indicates that
98% of students report their school has safe buildings and safe places to
play; 98% of students report their school has areas that meet their needs
(classroom, library, playground, cafeteria). The goals of this action is to
maintain a record of "No Deficiencies" on SARC reports in the area of
District facilities maintained and in good repair.

goals.description:
Goal 4: Attendance and Participation
Magnolia School District will enhance supports and strategies to increase student attendance and
participation in school to address chronic absenteeism.

actions.title: Attendance Recovery
actions.description:
P r o vide Attendance Recovery opportunities for all students. Encourage
students who are chronically absent to attend all dates. Provide engaging
activities that support learning recovery and high levels of engagement
with a variety of activities during after school activities.

actions.title:
Student Attendance
Review Board
(SARB)
actions.description:
Revise the Student Attendance Review Board (SARB) process to include
more intervention opportunities for families and students. Preventive
measures support students who may be on the path to chronic
absenteeism. A few of the updates include but are not limited to
implementing bi-weekly attendance reports, completing truancy
intervention document for Student Attendance Review Team (SART)
meetings, and holding the SART meeting after Truancy Letter #2.

actions.title:
Student and
Attendance
Engagement Teams
actions.description:
Implement the Student and Attendance Engagement Teams to support
with parent and family outreach for students who are struggling with
c o nsistent attendance. These teams can include but are not limited to the
Parent Involvement Liaisons, Health Clerk Technicians, and Social
Workers at each school site and include Community Schools staff for those
schools who qualify.
Magnolia School District Community Schools provide targeted support in
family outreach for students who continue to struggle with consistent
attendance and the Community Schools Social Worker and Community
School Parent Involvement Liaisons provide additional resources and
support to families that need more enhanced supports. These staff
members will be part of the Student and Attendance Engagement Teams
at the schools that qualify for Community Schools.

actions.title:
Attendance
Handbook
actions.description:
Implement an Attendance Handbook to provide school staff guidance on
attendance processes and procedures to address chronic absenteeism.
